content:
HTML Web Development JavaScript Apple AI 3 min read 1 hour ago -- If you’re starting your web development journey, there are a few concepts you need to understand before writing your first line of code. In this article, we’ll cover the fundamentals of the web, how websites work, what HTML is, and the most common HTML elements you’ll use every day. Press enter or click to view image in full size Introduction to the Web The World Wide Web (WWW) is a collection of websites and web pages that you access through the internet using a web browser like Chrome, Firefox, or Edge. Every website you visit — from Google to YouTube — is built using web technologies such as: • HTML — Structures the content. • CSS — Styles the content. • JavaScript — Adds interactivity. Think of it like building a house: • HTML is the foundation and walls. • CSS is the paint and decoration. • JavaScript is the electricity and appliances. How Websites Work Whenever you visit a website, a simple process happens behind the scenes. • You enter a website address (URL). • Your browser sends a request to a web server. • The server processes the request. • The server returns HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and other files. • The browser renders the page for you to see. Browser → Server → HTML, CSS, JavaScript → Browser Displays WebsiteThis process usually happens in just a few milliseconds. What is HTML? HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard language used to create web pages. It doesn’t make websites beautiful — that’s CSS’s job. Instead, HTML provides the structure of a page by defining elements such as headings, paragraphs, images, links, forms, and buttons. A simple HTML page looks like this: My First Website

Hello, World!

Welcome to HTML.

HTML Document Structure Every HTML document follows the same basic structure. My Website Content goes here. What each part does • tells the browser you're using HTML5. • is the root element. • contains information about the page. • sets the browser tab title. • <body> contains everything visible on the webpage. HTML Tags and Elements An HTML tag is the markup inside angle brackets (< >). <p>An HTML element includes the opening tag, content, and closing tag. <p>This is a paragraph.</p>Most HTML elements follow this pattern: <opening-tag> Content </closing-tag>Some elements, like <img>, don't have closing tags. <img src="photo.jpg" alt="Photo"> Headings HTML provides six heading levels. <h1>Main Heading</h1> <h2>Section Heading</h2> <h3>Subheading</h3> <h4>Smaller Heading</h4> <h5>Small Heading</h5> <h6>Smallest Heading</h6>Use headings to organize your content. Typically, each page should have one <h1>. Paragraphs Paragraphs display blocks of text. <p> HTML is the foundation of every website. </p>Each paragraph starts on a new line automatically. Comments Comments are notes for developers. They are ignored by the browser and aren’t visible on the webpage. <!-- This is a comment -->Comments are useful for: • Explaining code • Leaving reminders • Temporarily disabling code during testing Example: <!-- Navigation starts here --><nav> ... </nav> Final Thoughts Before moving on to CSS and JavaScript, make sure you’re comfortable with these HTML fundamentals: • Understanding how the web works • Knowing what HTML is • Recognizing the structure of an HTML document • Understanding the difference between tags and elements • Using headings, paragraphs, and comments correctly Once you’ve mastered these basics, you’ll have a solid foundation for building modern websites.
